title: The Keddie Cabin Murders — 43 Years of Unanswered Questions
description: On April 11, 1981, four people were found brutally murdered inside Cabin 28 of the Keddie resort in Northern California. A fifth victim — 12-year-old Tina Sharp — was missing. Three years later, her skull was found 50 miles away. The case was mishandled from the start, the evidence was lost, and no one has ever been convicted. Forty-three years later, it remains officially unsolved.

segments.voice: April 12, 1981. Keddie, California. A small resort community in the Sierra Nevada mountains, 90 miles north of Sacramento. The Sharpe family — Sue Sharpe, 36, and her children — have been living in Cabin 28 since the previous autumn. On this Sunday morning, 14-year-old Sheila Sharpe goes to check on her mother. She finds the front door unlocked. Inside is a scene that will define Plumas County for the next four decades.
segments.visual: Sierra Nevada mountains California resort cabin 1981

segments.voice: Sue Sharpe, 36. Her son John, 15. His friend Dana Wingate, 17. They were murdered — bound with tape and medical tubing, beaten with a hammer, stabbed with a kitchen knife and a serrated bread knife. The violence was extreme and prolonged, suggesting either multiple attackers or a single attacker in a sustained frenzy. Twelve-year-old Tina Sharp, Sue's youngest daughter, was not in the cabin. She was gone.
segments.visual: crime scene cabin interior forensic investigation brutal

segments.voice: Sheila had survived because she had slept over at a neighboring cabin. Three younger siblings — Ricky, Greg, and Justin — had also been in Cabin 28 that night, sleeping in a back bedroom. They were physically unharmed. What they heard — or what they repressed of what they heard — became one of the most painful subplots of the case. The children lived with the trauma for decades.
segments.visual: surviving children siblings testimony trauma decades

segments.voice: The investigation was conducted by Plumas County Sheriff's Department — a small department without the resources or the experience to handle a crime scene of this magnitude. The cabin was not properly sealed. People entered and contaminated the scene. Evidence was handled improperly. Over the following years, key pieces of evidence went missing — including some of the original crime scene photographs and physical items collected from the cabin.
segments.visual: small town sheriff department crime scene contamination

segments.voice: Three years later, in April 1984, the skull and partial remains of Tina Sharp were found at a campsite near Feather Falls, Butte County — approximately 50 miles from Keddie. A different county. A different jurisdiction. The remains were examined and confirmed as Tina's. But how she had gotten there, who had killed her, and whether she had survived longer than the cabin victims was impossible to determine from the remains.
segments.visual: skull discovery 1984 Feather Falls 50 miles away

segments.voice: Over the years, multiple suspects were named in public discourse. One figure has consistently reappeared: Martin Smartt, a resident of the Keddie resort at the time of the murders who had a known history of violence and drug use. His former wife gave investigators a statement in which she claimed Smartt had confessed to her. Smartt died in 2000 without being charged.
segments.visual: suspect Martin Smartt resort resident history violence

segments.voice: A second potential accomplice, John 'Bo' Boubede, who had been staying with Smartt at the time, also died without being charged. Items belonging to the victims were found in the possession of people connected to these men. A hammer that may have been the murder weapon was allegedly recovered and then lost by investigators. The chain of custody failures in this case are extraordinary.
segments.visual: accomplice Boubede hammer lost evidence chain custody

segments.voice: Sheila Sharpe, who discovered the bodies, has been a persistent presence in the case's public life. She has given interviews, maintained pressure on investigators, and never accepted that the case was a lower priority. Her testimony about what she knew, what she suspected, and what was done and not done by investigators has been crucial to public understanding of the investigation's failures.
segments.visual: survivor Sheila Sharpe advocate interview persistence

segments.voice: In 2016, the Plumas County Sheriff's Department made a public announcement: they had identified two suspects — Martin Smartt and Bo Boubede — as the people they believed responsible for the murders. Both were dead. No charges could be filed. This represented a formal institutional conclusion without any legal accountability. For the survivors, it was both a partial vindication and a profound disappointment.
segments.visual: 2016 announcement suspects identified dead no charges

segments.voice: The announcement in 2016 was followed by questions about why it had taken 35 years to reach this conclusion. The answer, investigators acknowledged, involved the early evidence failures, the small department's limitations, the inter-county complications created by Tina's remains being found in a different jurisdiction, and years of under-resourced cold case work.
segments.visual: 35 years delay systemic failure cold case review

segments.voice: The cabin itself was demolished by the resort's owner in 2004, citing its condition and the liability created by its dark reputation. The demolition was controversial — some investigators and advocates argued that physical evidence might still have been present in the structure. The site is now an empty lot at the Keddie resort, which itself barely survives as a community.
segments.visual: cabin 28 demolished 2004 empty lot Keddie resort

segments.voice: Keddie is almost a ghost town today. The resort has struggled financially for decades. Many of the cabins are empty or in disrepair. The small community that existed there in 1981 — with its mixture of permanent residents and transient visitors — has largely dispersed. The place that produced one of California's most notorious unsolved crimes has itself almost ceased to exist.
segments.visual: Keddie resort ghost town today empty cabins decay

segments.voice: True crime researchers and journalists have investigated the Keddie case for decades. The blog and subsequent book by journalist Erin Allen generated new attention in the 2010s. Online investigators created detailed timelines, analyzed the forensic evidence, and questioned the 2016 sheriff's conclusion. Some believe additional suspects may have been involved — that two men alone may not account for all the evidence.
segments.visual: true crime journalist blog research online investigation

segments.voice: The surviving children — Sheila, Ricky, Greg, and Justin Sharpe — have carried this case their entire adult lives. They lost their mother and their sister in a single night. They grew up defined by a crime that was never prosecuted, by an investigation that was compromised from the start, and by the knowledge that the men most likely responsible died without facing justice.
segments.visual: surviving family siblings adult lives grief weight

segments.voice: Sue Sharpe was 36. John Sharpe was 15. Dana Wingate was 17. Tina Sharp was 12. They were killed in the mountains of Northern California on an April night in 1981. No one has ever been convicted of their murders. The cabin is gone. The suspects are dead. The evidence is compromised. And somewhere in the Sierra Nevada, in the space between what happened and what could be proven, the truth of Cabin 28 remains.
segments.visual: Sierra Nevada mountains memorial cabin 28 sunset truth
